subject: Internal medicine
title: Lipid disorders
preambleMarkdown:
# Lipid disorders



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Lipid disorders is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Lipid disorders to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Lipid disorders is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Lipid disorders depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Lipid disorders.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Lipid disorders disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Lipid disorders should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Lipid disorders first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Lipid disorders, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Lipid disorders, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Lipid disorders presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Lipid disorders with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Lipid disorders is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Lipid disorders should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Lipid disorders is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Lipid disorders, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Lipid disorders may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Lipid disorders patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Lipid disorders, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Lipid disorders in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Lipid disorders, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Lipid disorders does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Lipid disorders may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Lipid disorders, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Lipid disorders as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Lipid disorders draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Hyperlipidemias

_Source lines: 39499-39873._

```text
Hyperlipidemias
A. General Characteristics
1. Hyperlipidemia is one of the most important (and modifiable) risk
factors for CAD. It causes accelerated atherosclerosis
2. Hyperlipidemia may be a primary disorder, such as a familial

dyslipidemia syndrome, or secondary to another cause

## 3. Classification of dyslipidemia syndromes-types IIA, IIB, and IV

account for over 80% of all of familial dyslipidemias (Table 12-2)
CLINICAL PEARL 12-1
Risk Factors for CAD in Evaluation of Patients With
Hyperlipidemia
- Current cigarette smoking (dose-dependent risk)
- HTN
- Diabetes mellitus
- Low HDL cholesterol (<35 mg/dL); high HDL (>60 mg/dL) is a negative
risk factor (subtract 1 from total)
- Age
- Male: >45 years of age
- Female: >55 years of age
- Male gender-if you count as a risk factor, do not count age
- Family history of premature CAD
- MI/sudden death in male first-degree relative <55 years of age
- MI/sudden death in female first-degree relative <65 years of age
1. Secondary causes of hyperlipidemia
a. Endocrine disorders-hypothyroidism, DM, Cushing syndrome
b. Renal disorders-nephrotic syndrome, uremia
c. Chronic liver disease
d. Medications-glucocorticoids, estrogen, thiazide diuretics, beta-
blockers
e. Pregnancy
2. Risk factors
a. Diet
- Saturated fatty acids and cholesterol cause elevation in LDL
and total cholesterol
- High-calorie diets do not increase LDL or cholesterol levels
(are "neutral") but do increase triglyceride (TG) levels
- Alcohol increases TG levels and HDL levels but does not
affect total cholesterol levels
e. Age-cholesterol levels increase with age until approximately
age 65 years. The increase is greatest during early adulthood-
about 2 mg/dL per year
f. Inactive lifestyle, abdominal obesity
g. Family history of hyperlipidemia

h. Gender-men generally have higher cholesterol levels than
women; when women reach menopause, cholesterol levels then
equalize and may even be higher in women than in men
i. Medications
- Thiazides-increase LDL, total cholesterol, TG (VLDL) levels
- beta-Blockers (propranolol)-increase TGs (VLDL) and lower
HDL levels
- Estrogens-TG levels may be further increased in patients
with hypertriglyceridemia
- Corticosteroids and HIV protease inhibitors can elevate
serum lipids
n. Genetic mutations that predispose to the most severe
hyperlipidemias
o. Secondary causes of dyslipidemia (see above)
TABLE 12-2 Dyslipidemia Syndromes
Class 	Name 	Lipoprotein
Elevated
Treatment
Type I 	Exogenous hyperlipidemia 	Chylomicrons 	Diet
Type
IIa
Familial hypercholesterolemia 	LDL 	Statins
Niacin
Cholestyramine
Type
IIb
Combined
hyperlipoproteinemia
LDL + VLDL 	Statins
Niacin
Gemfibrozil
Type III 	Familial
dysbetalipoproteinemia
IDL 	Gemfibrozil
Niacin
Type IV 	Endogenous hyperlipidemia 	VLDL 	Niacin
Gemfibrozil
Statins
Type V 	Familial hypertriglyceridemia 	VLDL +
chylomicrons
Niacin
Gemfibrozil
IDL, intermediate density lipoprotein.

FIGURE
12-3 Progression of atherosclerotic plaque formation and rupture.

(Reprinted with permission from Golan DE, Tashjian AH, Armstrong EJ, et al. Principles of
Pharmacology: The Pathophysiologic Basis of Drug Therapy. 3rd ed. Wolters Kluwer Health/
Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2011. Figure 25-7.)

1. Role of lipids in CAD risk
a. LDL cholesterol
- Accounts for two-thirds of total cholesterol. CAD risk is
primarily due to the LDL component because LDL is the
most atherogenic of all lipoproteins (Figure 12-3).

- LDL cholesterol is not directly measured. It is calculated as
follows: LDL = total cholesterol - HDL - TG/5
d. Total cholesterol
- The risk of CAD increases sharply when total cholesterol is
above 240 mg/dL
f. HDL cholesterol
- Its protective effect (removes excess cholesterol from arterial
walls) is at least as strong as the atherogenic effect of LDL
- For every 10 mg/dL increase in HDL levels, CAD risk
decreases by 50%
- High HDL (>60 mg/dL) is a "negative" risk factor
(counteracts one risk factor) for CAD

j. The total cholesterol-to-HDL ratio-the lower the total
cholesterol-to-HDL ratio, the lower the risk of CAD
- Ratio of 5.0 is average (standard) risk
- Ratio of 10 is double the risk
- Ratio of 20 is triple the risk
- Ratio of <4.5 is desirable
o. TGs-elevated TGs are associated with coronary risk, but it is
unknown whether this association is causal. It is uncertain
whether lowering TG level reduces coronary risk.

Quick HIT
Estrogen Replacement Therapy in Postmenopausal Women
The HERS trial showed no benefit of hormone replacement therapy
on cardiovascular outcomes in women with established CHD.

However, the study did not address the issue in women without
CHD. The results are somewhat controversial.

A 49-year-old man presents for routine physical examination. The patient
has no active complaints today other than occasional constipation and
increased frequency of urination. The patient reports that he has a
diagnosis of "pre-diabetes" given to him by his previous physician in
another city. His family history is significant for hypertension. The
patient denies alcohol use but smokes 12 pack of cigarettes daily. He
works as a long-distance truck driver. Physical examination is
unremarkable except for a body mass index of 31 kg/m 2 . The patient's
fasting laboratory values reveal the following.

Hemoglobin A1c 	7.9%
Serum glucose 	170 mg/dL
Total cholesterol 	220 mg/dL
Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) 	145 mg/dL
Triglycerides 	130 mg/dL
High-density lipoprotein 	38 mg/dL
Which of the following is the best next step in the management of
this patient?

A. Lifestyle modification
B. Atorvastatin and lifestyle modification

C. Sulfonylurea
D. Niacin, fish oil, and lifestyle modification
- The answer is B: Atorvastatin and lifestyle modification. This
patient is presenting with a new diagnosis of diabetes mellitus
(T2DM) based on his hemoglobin A1c. Patients with diabetes are at
significant risk for cardiovascular atherosclerotic events. Based on his
risk factors and lipid screen, he is at an elevated risk (>10%) of
cardiovascular events and should begin statin therapy. Risk score
calculators are available for this purpose. (C) A sulfonylurea (e.g.,
glimepiride) is not the initial drug of choice with new-onset diabetes.

Metformin therapy is recommended after diagnosing T2DM. (D)
Niacin is helpful in raising high-density lipoprotein, but may actually
worsen glucose control in diabetics. Fish oil is helpful in reducing
serum triglycerides but does not have an effect on improving
cardiovascular outcomes.

B. Clinical Features
1. Most patients are asymptomatic
2. The following may be manifestations of severe hyperlipidemia:
a. Xanthelasma-yellow plaques on eyelids.

b. Xanthoma-hard, yellowish masses found on tendons (finger
extensors, Achilles tendon, plantar tendons)
3. Pancreatitis can occur with severe hypertriglyceridemia
Quick HIT
Statins have been shown to significantly reduce rates of MI, stroke,
and coronary and all-cause mortality in prospective placebo-
controlled trials.

C. Diagnosis
1. Lipid screening (see Health Maintenance section)-measure total
cholesterol and HDL levels (nonfasting is acceptable). If either is
abnormal, then order a full fasting lipid profile
2. A full fasting lipid profile includes TG levels and calculation of LDL
levels
3. Consider checking laboratory tests to exclude secondary causes of
hyperlipidemia
a. TSH (hypothyroidism)
b. LFTs (chronic liver disease)
c. BUN and Cr, urinary proteins (nephrotic syndrome)
d. Glucose levels (diabetes)

D. Treatment
1. Recent pharmaceutical advances and guidelines (including ACC/
AHA) have dramatically changed the treatment of dyslipidemias in
the last decade. See Table 12-3.

a. The long-term goal is the same: to reduce morbidity and
mortality from CAD by promoting a healthy diet and lifestyle
throughout life.

b. Statins are still the first-line treatment for patients with
atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) or at high risk.

Indications for starting a statin include established ASCVD, LDL
levels of 190 mg/dL or higher, diabetic patients aged 40 to 75
years with LDL level of 70 mg/dL or higher, or all patients aged
40 to 75 years with ASCVD risk of at least 7.5% or higher (using
the ACC/AHA ASCVD risk calculator).

c. If LDL remains >100 mg/dL despite statin therapy, consider
adding ezetimibe.

d. If LDL remains >100 mg/dL despite statin + ezetimibe,
consider adding a PCSK9 inhibitor (e.g., evolocumab,
alirocumab). This can be considered also in the case of statin
intolerance.

e. Dietary therapy is important before and during statin therapy.

Lowering fat intake (especially saturated fats) reduces serum
cholesterol more than lowering cholesterol intake. Foods rich in
omega-3 fatty acids (such as fish) are particularly beneficial.

With an intensive diet, LDL can be reduced by an average of
10%, as follows: <30% of total calories from fat; with fewer
than 10% from saturated fat; <300 mg/day of cholesterol.

f. Exercise and weight loss-reduce risk of CAD.

- Exercise increases HDL and reduces other CAD risk factors
by lowering BP and enhancing the eficiency of peripheral
oxygen extraction.

- Weight loss reduces myocardial work as well as the risk of
diabetes.

i. Smoking cessation
TABLE 12-3 Updated Cholesterol Treatment Guidelines
Cholesterol Treatment
to Reduce
Cardiovascular Events
Category 	Recommended Therapy

Clinical ASCVDa 	High-intensity statin. Can add ezetimibe if LDL still
above 70 mg/dL after statin. Can add PCSK9
inhibitor if still above 70 m/dL.

LDL cholesterol >=190 mg/
dL
High-intensity statin for ages 20-75 yrs. Can add
ezetimibe if LDL reduction not at least 50%, or still
above 100 mg/dL. Consider bile acid sequestrant if
TGs >300 mg/dL. PCSK9 inhibitors can be
considered if above goals not met.

Diabetic patients aged
40-75 yrs
Moderate-intensity statin for all patients, high-
intensity statin for those with ASCVD risk factors. If
ASCVD score >20% can consider adding ezetimibe.

Nondiabetic patients
aged 40-75 yrs with LDL
cholesterol 70-189 mg/
dL
ASCVD risk score >=7.5%, consider moderate-
intensity statin. If >20%, then start a high-intensity
statin.

aASCVD, atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, which includes patients with acute
coronary syndromes or a history of MI, stable or unstable angina, coronary or other arterial
revascularization, stroke, TIA, or peripheral arterial disease from atherosclerosis.

Modified from Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC/AACVPR/AAPA/
ABC/ACPM/ADA/AGS/APhA/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Guideline on the Management of Blood
Cholesterol: A Report of the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association
Task Force on Clinical Practice Guidelines. J Am Coll Cardiol 2019;73(24):e285--e350.

Copyright  2019 by the American Heart Association, Inc., and the American College of
Cardiology Foundation. With permission.

Quick HIT
Potency of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors ("statins"):
- High intensity: atorvastatin 40 to 80 mg, rosuvastatin 20 to 40
mg
- Moderate intensity: atorvastatin 10 to 20 mg, rosuvastatin 5 to
10 mg, simvastatin 20 to 40 mg, lovastatin 40 to 80 mg,
pravastatin 40 to 80 mg, fluvastatin XL 80 mg (or 40 mg twice
daily), pitavastatin 1 to 4 mg
- Low intensity: simvastatin 10 mg, pravastatin 10 to 20 mg,
lovastatin 20 mg, fluvastatin 20 to 40 mg
Quick HIT
It is no longer recommended to routinely monitor AST and ALT after
starting a statin.

A 39-year-old man presents for his annual physical examination. He has
a history of asthma, pre-hypertension, and Gilbert syndrome. The patient
smokes a pack of cigarettes per day, but is pleased because he has cut it
down from 2.5 packs per day. He has smoked since the age of 20 years.

The patient is motivated at this visit to seek assistance in smoking
cessation.

Which of the following medications can help him quit smoking in both
the short and long term?

A. Propranolol
B. Buspirone
C. Bupropion
D. Paroxetine
- The answer is C: Bupropion. Bupropion is a drug primarily used as
an atypical antidepressant and is used in smoking cessation. Though
varenicline is not listed, it is another agent used for smoking
cessation that likely even more effective than bupropion. Varenicline
acts via partial agonism of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors.

Nicotine-replacement therapy, either alone or in addition to
bupropion or varenicline, can also be used and may be more effective
than using either medication alone. Bupropion is unique compared to
the other antidepressant drugs in that it has no sexual side effects. It
should not be used in bulimic patients and those at risk of seizures
because it can lower the seizure threshold.

1. Therapy for high TG levels-the data are fairly limited regarding
which patients should be treated and which medication to use.

a. First-line therapy is weight loss, aerobic exercise, glycemic
control in diabetics, and low-fat diet. Typically patients will be
on a medication to lower LDL levels (e.g., statin), which will
also help lower TG levels. However, if TG levels remain
elevated, additional medications can be considered. This
decision is typically based on ASCVD risk factors, history of
pancreatitis from hypertriglyceridemia, etc.

b. Medications include icosapent ethyl, fibrates, nicotinic acid
(niacin), and fish oil.

Quick HIT
Emergency Evaluation of Headache
- Obtain a noncontrast CT scan to first rule out any type of
intracranial bleed.

- However, small bleeds (subarachnoid hemorrhage) may be
missed by CT scan, so a lumbar puncture may be necessary (to

potentially visualize the presence of blood breakdown
products).
```
